reason: The text describes an explicit and sustained conflict between the Hebrew people (בְנֵ֣י יִשְׂרָאֵ֔ל) and Pharaoh, who represents the Egyptian ruling elite. Moses and Aaron, speaking on behalf of the enslaved Hebrews, repeatedly confront Pharaoh demanding the release of the Hebrew people from slavery ('שַׁלַּ֥ח אֶת־ עַמִּ֖י' - 'let my people go'). The Hebrews are explicitly enslaved laborers, making them 'workers.' This conflict persists throughout the narrative as Pharaoh repeatedly refuses their pleas despite the escalating plagues, until finally agreeing to their departure. The phrase 'יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵ֣י הָֽעִבְרִ֔ים' (The Lord, God of the Hebrews) emphasizes the identity of those in conflict with the Egyptian authorities.
